summary:
The founding of the Goryeo Dynasty (918–1392 AD) by Wang Geon unified the Korean peninsula under a new dynasty following the collapse of the Later Three Kingdoms period, establishing a state whose name — Goryeo — would give the English word 'Korea.' Wang Geon, a general who had risen to power in the Later Goguryeo kingdom, proclaimed the new dynasty in 918 AD and completed unification by absorbing Silla in 935 AD and Later Baekje in 936 AD, ending decades of fragmentation.

The Goryeo Dynasty represented a high point of Korean civilization, producing achievements that endured for centuries. Korean celadon pottery — prized for its distinctive jade-green glaze — achieved technical refinements during Goryeo that were admired even by Song Dynasty Chinese connoisseurs. The dynasty also oversaw the creation of the Tripitaka Koreana (completed 1251), the most complete and accurate collection of Buddhist scriptures in the world, carved onto 81,258 wooden printing blocks that survive intact to this day at Haeinsa Temple. Goryeo developed a sophisticated civil service examination system modeled on Tang and Song Chinese institutions, creating a meritocratic bureaucracy that would shape Korean governance for centuries. The dynasty faced severe pressure from Mongol invasions beginning in 1231, eventually becoming a Mongol vassal state while maintaining nominal independence — a period that introduced both trauma and new cultural influences.
